volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Captives (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.2.173b)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Hos quos videtis stare hic captivos duos ,
sections.translation: THESE two captives ( pointing to PHILOCRATES and TYNDARUS ) , whom you see standing here , are standing here because — they are both [*] standing , and are not sitting . That I am saying this truly , you are my witnesses . The old man , who lives here ( pointing to HEGIO ’ S house ) , is Hegio — his father ( pointing to TYNDARUS .)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: sed is quo pacto serviat suo sibi patri ,
sections.translation: But under what circumstances he is the slave of his own father , that I will here explain to you , if you give attention . This old man had two sons ; a slave stole one child when four years old , and flying hence , he sold him in Elis [*] ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: patri huius . iam hoc tenetis ? ⋯ optume est .
sections.translation: to the father of this captive ( pointing to PHIILOCRATES .) Now , do you understand this ? Very good . I ’ faith , that man at a distance [*] there ( pointing ) says , no . Come nearer then . If there isn ’ t room for you to sit down , there is for you to walk ; since you ’ d be compelling an actor to bawl like a beggar [*] . I ’ m not going to burst myself for your sake , so don ’ t you be mistaken .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: vos qui potestis ope vestra censerier ,
sections.translation: You who are enabled by your means to pay your taxes [*] , listen to the rest [*] ; I care not to be in debt to another . This runaway slave , as I said before , sold his young master , whom , whom he fled , he had carried off , to this one ’ s father . He , after he bought him , gave him as his own

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: peculiarem , quia quasi una aetas erat .
sections.translation: private slave [*] to his son of his , because they were of about the same age . He is now the slave at home of his own father , nor does his father know it . Verily , the Gods do treat us men just like footballs [*] . You hear the manner now how he lost one son . Afterwards , the Aetolians [*] are waging war with the people of Elis ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: ut fit in bello , capitur alter filius :
sections.translation: and , as happens in warfare , the other son is taken prisoner . The physician Menarchus buys him there in Elis . On this , this Hegio begins to traffic in Elean captives , if , perchance , he may be able to find one to change for that captive son of his . He knows not that this one who is in his house is his own son .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: et quoniam heri indaudivit , de summo loco
sections.translation: And as he heard yesterday that an Elean knight of very high rank and very high family was taken prisoner , he has spared no expense to rescue his son [*] . In order that he may more easily bring him back home , he buys both of these of the Quaestors [*] out of the spoil .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: hisce autem inter sese hunc confinxerunt dolum ,
sections.translation: Now they , between themselves , have contrived this plan , that , by means of it , the servant may send away hence his master home . And therefore among themselves they change their garments and their names . He , there ( pointing ) , is called Philocrates ; this one ( pointing ) , Tyndarus ; he this day assumes the character of this one , this one of him .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: et hic hódie expediet hanc docte fallaciam ,
sections.translation: And this one to-day will cleverly carry out this plot , and cause his master to gain his liberty ; and by the same means he will save his own brother , and without knowing it , will cause him to return back a free man to his own country to his father . Just as often now , on many occasions ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.45
sections.original: plus insciens quis fecit quam prudens boni .
sections.translation: a person has done more good unknowingly than knowingly . But unconsciously , by their devices , they have so planned and devised their plot , and have so contrived it by their design , that this one is living in servitude with his own father .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: ita nunc ignorans suo sibi servit patri ;
sections.translation: And thus now , in ignorance , he is the slave of his own father . What poor creatures are men , when I reflect upon it ! This plot will be performed by us — a play for your entertainment . But there is , besides , a thing which , in a few words , I would wish to inform you of . Really , it will be worth your while to give your attention to this play .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: non pertractate facta est neque item ut ceterae :
sections.translation: ’ Tis not composed in the hackneyed style , nor yet like other plays , nor are there in it any ribald lines [*] unfit for utterance : here is neither the perjured procurer , nor the artful courtesan , nor yet the braggart captain . Don ’ t you be afraid because I ’ ve said that there ’ s war between the Aetolians and the Eleans .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: foris íllic extra scaenam fient proelia .
sections.translation: There ( pointing ) , at a distance , beyond the scenes , the battles will be fought . For this were almost impossible for a Comic establishmen [*] , that we should at a moment attempt to be acting Tragedy . If , therefore , any one is looking for a battle , let him commence the quarrel ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.65
sections.original: si erit , ego faciam ut pugnam inspectet non bonam ,
sections.translation: if he shall find an adversary more powerful , I ’ ll cause him to be the spectator of a battle that isn ’ t pleasant to him , so that hereafter he shall hate to be a spectator them all . I now retire . Fare ye well , at home , most upright judges , and in warfare most valiant combatants .

sections.label: Section 1.1.69
sections.original: Iuventus nomen indidit Scorto mihi ,
sections.translation: The young men have given me the name of the mistress ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: eo quía invocatus soleo esse in convivio .
sections.translation: for this reason , because invocated [*] I am wont to attend at the banquet . I know that buffoons [*] say that this is absurdly said , but I affirm that it is rightly said . For at the banquet the lover , when he throws the dice , invokes his mistress [*] . Is she then invocated , or is she not ? She is , most clearly .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: verum hercle vero nos parasiti planius ,
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , we Parasites with better reason are so called , whom no person ever either invites or invokes , and who , like mice , are always eating the victuals of another person . When business is laid aside [*] , when people repair to the country , at that same moment is business laid aside for our teeth .

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: quasi , cum caletur , cocleae in occulto latent ,
sections.translation: Just as , when it is hot weather , snails lie hidden in secret , and live upon their own juices , if the dew doesn ’ t fall ; so , when business is laid aside , do Parasites lie hidden in retirement , and miserably live upon their own juices , while in the country the persons are rusticating whom they sponge upon .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: prolatis rebus parasiti venatici
sections.translation: When business is laid aside , we Parasites are greyhounds ; when business recommences , like mastiffs [*] , we are annoying-like and very troublesome-like [*] . And here , indeed , unless , i ’ faith , any Parasite is able to endure cuffs with the fist , and pots to be broken [*] about his head ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: vel ire extra portam Trigeminam ad saccum licet .
sections.translation: why he may e ’ en go with his wallet outside the Trigeminian Gate [*] . That this may prove my lot , there is some danger . For since my patron [*] has fallen into the hands of the enemy —( such warfare are the Aetolians now waging with the Eleans ; for this is Aetolia ; this Philopolemus has been made captive in Elis , the son of this old man Hegio

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: Philopolemus , huius Hegionis filius
sections.translation: who lives here ( pointing to the house ) — a house which to me is a house of woe , and which so oft as I look upon , I weep ). Now , for the sake of his son , has he commenced this dishonorable traffic , very much against his own inclination .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: homines captivos commercatur , si queat
sections.translation: He buys up men that have been made captives , if perchance he may be able to find some one for whom to gain his son in exchange . An object which I really do much desire that he may gain . For unless he finds him , there ’ s nowhere for me to find myself . I have no hopes in the young men ; they are all too fond of themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: ille démum antiquis est adulescens moribus ,
sections.translation: He , in fine , is a youth with the old-fashioned nanners , whose countenance I never rendered cheerful without a return . His father is worthily matched , as endowed with like manners . Now I ’ ll go to him ;— but his door is opening , the door from which full oft I ’ ve sallied forth drunk with excess of cheer .

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: Advorte animum sis tu : istos captivos duos ,
sections.translation: Now , give attention you , if you please . Those two captives whom I purchased yesterday of the Quaestors out of the spoil , put upon them chains of light weight [*] ; take off those greater ones with which they are bound . Permit them to walk , if they wish , out of doors , or if in-doors ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: sed uti adserventur magna diligentia .
sections.translation: but so that they are watched with the greatest care . A captive at liberty is like a bird that ’ s wild ; if opportunity is once given for escaping , ‘ tis enough ; after that , you can never catch him .

sections.label: Section 1.2.119
sections.original: Omnes profecto liberi lubentius
sections.translation: Doubtless we all are free men more willingly than we live the life of slaves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120b
sections.original: Non videre ita tu quidem .
sections.translation: You , indeed , don ’ t seem to think so [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.original: Si non est quod dem , mene vis dem ipse — in pedes ?
sections.translation: If I have nothing to give , should you like me to give myself to flight [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.122
sections.original: Si dederis , erit extemplo mihi quod dem tibi .
sections.translation: If you do so give yourself , I shall at once have something to be giving to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123
sections.original: Avis mé ferae consimilem faciam , ut praedicas .
sections.translation: I ’ ll make myself just like the wild bird you were telling of .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Ita ut dícis : nam si faxis , te in caveam dabo .
sections.translation: ’ Tis just as you say ; for if you do so , I ’ ll be giving you to the cage [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: sed satis verborumst . cura quae iussi atque abi .
sections.translation: But enough of prating ; take you care of what I ’ ve ordered , and be off . ( The SLAVE goes into the house .) I ’ ll away to my brother ’ s , to my other captives ; I ’ ll go see whether they ’ ve been making any disturbance last night . From there I shall forthwith betake myself home again .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: Aegre est mi , hunc facere quaestum carcerarium
sections.translation: It grieves me that this unhappy old man is following the trade of a slave-dealer ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: propter sui gnati miseriam miserum senem .
sections.translation: by reason of the misfortune of his son . But , if by any means he can be brought back here , I could even endure for him to become an executioner .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Quis hic lóquitur ?
sections.translation: Who is it that ’ s speaking ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133b
sections.original: Ego , qui tuo maerore maceror ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis I , who am , pining at your affliction , growing thin , waxing old , and shockingly wasting away . Wretched man that I am ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: ossa atque pellis sum miser a macritudine ;
sections.translation: I ’ m but skin and bone through leanness ; nor does anything ever do me good that I eat at home ; even that ever so little which I taste out of doors , the same refreshes me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: Ergasile , salve .
sections.translation: Ergasilus , save you !

sections.label: Section 1.2.138b
sections.original: Di te bene ament , Hegio .
sections.translation: May the Gods kindly bless you , Hegio !

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Ne fle .
sections.translation: Don ’ t weep .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139b
sections.original: Egone illum non fleam ? égon non defleam
sections.translation: Must I not weep for him ? Must I not weep for such a young man ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.140b
sections.original: Semper sensi , filio
sections.translation: I ’ ve always known you to be a friend to my son , and I have understood him to be so to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.142
sections.original: Tum denique homines nostra intellegimus bona ,
sections.translation: Then at last do we men know our blessings , when we have lost those things which we once had in our power . I , since your son fell into the power of the enemy ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: expertus quanti fuerit nunc desidero .
sections.translation: knowing by experience of what value he was , now feel his loss .

sections.label: Section 1.2.146
sections.original: Alienus cum eius incommodum tam aegre feras ,
sections.translation: Since you , who are no relation , bear his misfortune so much amiss , what is it likely that I , a father , should do , whose only son he is ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.148
sections.original: Alienus ego ? alienus illi ? aha , Hegio ,
sections.translation: I , no relation to him ? He , no relation to me ? Oh , Hegio ! never do say that , nor come to such a belief .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: tibi ille únicust , mi etiam unico magis unicus .
sections.translation: To you he is an only child , but to me he is even more only than an only one .

sections.label: Section 1.2.151
sections.original: Laudo , malum cum amici tuom ducis malum .
sections.translation: I commend you , in that you consider the affliction of your friend your own affliction . Now be of good heart .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152b
sections.original: Eheu , huic illud dolet ,
sections.translation: O dear !

sections.label: Section 1.2.153
sections.original: quia nunc remissus est edendi exercitus .
sections.translation: ’ Tis this afflicts him , [*] that the army for guttling is now disbanded .

sections.label: Section 1.2.154
sections.original: Nullumne interea nactu'snactus es , qui posset tibi
sections.translation: Meanwhile , have you found no one to command for you the army that you mentioned as disbanded ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.156
sections.original: Quid credis ? fugitant omnes hanc provinciam ,
sections.translation: What do you think ? All to whom it used to fall are in the habit of declining that province since your son Philopolemus was taken prisoner .

sections.label: Section 1.2.158
sections.original: Non pol mirandum est fugitare hanc provinciam .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , ‘ tisn ’ t to be wondered at , that they are in the habit of declining that province . You have necessity for numerous troops , and those of numerous kinds .

sections.label: Section 1.2.160
sections.original: militibus : primumdum opus est Pistorensibus ;
sections.translation: Well , first you have need of the Bakerians [*] . Of these Bakerians there are several kinds , You have need of Roll-makerians , you have need too of Confectionerians , you have need of Poultererians , you have need of Beccaficorians ; besides , all the maritime forces are necessary for you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.165
sections.original: Vt saepe summa ingenia in occulto latent ;
sections.translation: How the greatest geniuses do frequently lie concealed ! How great a general now is this private individual !

sections.label: Section 1.2.167
sections.original: Habe modo bonum animum , nam illum confido domum
sections.translation: Only have good courage ; for I trust that in a few days I shall bring him back home . For see now ; there ’ s a captive here , a young man of Elis ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.170
sections.original: prognatum genere summo et summis ditiis :
sections.translation: born of a very high family , and of very great wealth ; I trust that it will come to pass that I shall get my son in exchange for him .

sections.label: Section 1.2.172
sections.original: Ita di deaeque faxint . sed num quo foras
sections.translation: May the Gods and Goddesses grant it so !

sections.label: Section 1.2.173
sections.original: vocatus es ad cenam ?
sections.translation: But are you invited out anywhere to dinner ? [*]

sections.label: Section 1.2.173b
sections.original: Nusquam , quod sciam .
sections.translation: Nowhere that I know of . But , pray , why do you ask me ?
